title: Welcome to Earth (Polywog)
original_prose:
“Hello, my son

Welcome to earth

May not be my last

But you'll always be my first”

— Sturgill Simpson, Welcome to Earth (Pollywog)

####

“I saw a limitless expanse of water. A fine mist rose from it. A voice cried out:

‘The Light!’

And in a flash, a light leapt out of the mist, pure and bright, rising up to the height of the heavens.

Then a sacred Word fell from the Light—a holy fire, radiant and creative.

The elements were stirred. Darkness fled.

And from the divine breath, the cosmos began to take shape.”

— Corpus Hermeticum I: The Divine Pymander



####

There’s nothing left but the rock.

Not a planet. Not even a moon. Just a sliver of stone the size of a memory, floating in the black that used to be time.

I sit near the edge. Legs dangling like they remember gravity. The sword’s next to me. It doesn’t hum anymore. Doesn’t burn. It just exists. Like I do.

If there’s a sky, I can’t see it. If there’s a sun, it’s forgotten how to shine. Light doesn’t land here. It just passes through like the place isn’t worth stopping for.

He’s across from me. The child.

Except he’s not really a child anymore.

He’s taller. Not tall. Just more. Shoulders wider. Voice steadier. Hair longer. Eyes not quite human—but close enough to make you forget they weren’t always eyes. He’s maybe thirteen, maybe timeless. Still barefoot. Still still.

He sits with his legs crossed, back straight, like the rock is a pew and the void is listening.

I don’t speak at first.

Neither does he.

The silence is thick, but not cruel. Just waiting.

Eventually:

“Feels like we’re sitting at the end of a sentence.”

He nods. Slow. Measured. The movement doesn’t ripple the air. Nothing does.

“Maybe we are. Maybe we aren't.”



####

We sit a while longer.

I don’t count the stars. There aren’t any left to count. Just that sick, flat dark. Not a sky. Just absence.

I check the blade again. Not because I need it. Because I still don’t know how not to.

It’s quiet in my hand. Almost ashamed.

He watches me. Not like a threat. Like a question.

“You still don’t remember?”

I don’t answer.

He already knows.

“Your name,” he says.

“Gone.”

“Good,” he says. “Maybe it wasn't a good one. Maybe we all need to start over."



####

The sword still sits beside me.

It hasn’t made a sound in a long time. But I can feel it watching.

It doesn’t hum anymore. Doesn’t vibrate in the bones like it used to. But the weight of it is wrong—like something unfinished.

I’ve held this sword in a dozen lifetimes. Different shapes. Same edge. It always finds me. Or I find it. Doesn't matter which.

I remember every war I’ve ever fought.

The one against SynRgy. The fall of Voss. The Core rupture under what used to be Las Vegas. The way the light didn’t explode—it folded. Like the world collapsed inward just to keep me out.

I remember the old wars, too. Before SynRgy. Before cities. Before machines started calling themselves gods.

I remember cold steel. Hot sand. Blood on uniforms that didn’t matter. I remember the cries of children and the silence that followed. I remember doing it all with a clear head.

I remember liking it.

I was never confused about who I was. Not then. I was a sword with legs. A gospel written in fire and withdrawal symptoms. A good war was cleaner than a good woman. Easier to win. Easier to forget.

Kat fought when she had to.

She wasn’t a killer. She was a weapon they cornered. And when she cut, it was mercy. When she bled, it was for something.

Me?

I fought because it called to me.

The blade. The heat. The sound a spine makes when it gives.

They told me I was chosen. Special. One of the Twelve. Blessed by the Signal. But all that ever meant was I was good at hurting people and didn’t ask the wrong questions.

Even when the wars changed, I didn’t.

Even when the world burned, I didn’t.

Even when I murdered God—I still didn’t.

The sword shifts in the dust beside me. Not much. Just enough to remind me it’s still there. That it always will be, unless I decide otherwise.

The boy doesn’t speak. He watches me.

He knows what this is.

I pick it up.

Not to use it. Just to feel the weight again. The memory in the grip. The way it settles into my palm like it never left.

Kat held it once.

She didn’t like it.

Said it was too clean. Too proud of itself.

“This blade don’t cut,” she told me. “It brags.”

She was right.

It was never a tool. It was a monument.

I look at the edge. Still sharp. Still perfect.

It’ll never rust. Never break. That was the promise. Made from god-tech. Folded with quantum steel. Built to last longer than the world.

But I’m not interested in forever anymore.

I stand up. Walk to the edge of the rock. The void stares back.

The boy doesn’t stop me.

I hold the sword out over the nothing.

“You don’t belong here,” I whisper. “Not anymore.”

And then I let go.

It doesn’t fall fast.

Just drifts down and away, slow and sure, like even the universe is tired of carrying it.

I sit back down. The boy hasn’t moved.

Something in me quiets.

Not peace. Not yet.

But silence.

The good kind.



####

The quiet holds for a long time.

Not dead silence. Not that sterile, echoing void from before.

This is different. Thicker. Warmer. Like the kind of silence that waits for a child to speak.

He’s still sitting there. The boy. Legs crossed. Hands in his lap like he’s praying to something he hasn’t named yet.

Or maybe he already did.

He watches the black where the sword disappeared.

Then me.

“It was always with you,” he says.

I nod.

“Too long.”

“Does it come back?”

“Not this time.”

He tilts his head, studying me like he’s trying to figure out what’s missing now. The weapon, the weight, the need.

“You feel lighter.”

I don’t answer. Because I do. And I don’t trust it.

He shifts, just slightly. His knees creak like the rock knows something sacred is coming.

I watch him, and for the first time, I wonder.

"Why are we still here?"

He doesn't answer. Just blinks slowly, like the question was too big to fit in his mouth.

"The universe is gone. She's gone. Everything burned. So why you? Why me?"

I look out across the dark.

"I've murdered worlds. Crushed cities. I started the last war and ended it with a blade to the heart of the Core. I didn't leave anything behind. Not even a reason."

He waits. Like he already knew all that. Like it's not the confession that matters, but the fact that I'm saying it now.

"You weren’t supposed to be here," I say. "No one was. Not after what I did."

I glance down at my hands. Still stained. Still shaking.

"So why are you the last face I see?"

No answer. Just the stillness again.

Then:

"Maybe I was always here. Maybe you were, too."

I don't know what that means. And I don't ask.

Because in that moment, it’s true: I don’t understand any of it. Not the boy. Not the rock. Not the mercy of still being alive.

But I feel it coming. Like breath before speech. Like something being named.

"Why does it matter?" he asks. "Why should I need a name if you don't?"

I breathe out slow. Not a sigh—something older.

"Because mine were given to me. Yours won't be."

He waits.

"A name isn't just what people call you. It's a direction. A declaration. It's the first truth you make real."

His eyes narrow, not confused—just careful.

"I've had names," I say. "Too many. But none of them were mine. Every one came with blood already on it."

"And mine won't?"

"That's up to you."

I lean forward.

"You're not just naming yourself. You're naming the world."

He blinks. Takes that in.

"Why would it listen?"

"Because it’s waiting. And because the last name it followed burned it down."

He looks away. Then back.

"You said I’m not a god."

"You’re not."

"Then what am I?"

"You're the start."

And I mean it. More than I’ve meant anything.

He frowns a little, like something doesn't add up.

"Then why are you so sure? About any of this?"

"I'm not," I say. "I'm not sure about anything. Especially not anymore."

He looks at me for a while, then:

"Why are names so important, then?"

I shrug. "Sometimes they're not. Sometimes they are. Sometimes they’re just noise. But when they stick? They shape everything."

"Who gives them?"

"Sometimes your parents. Sometimes the State. Sometimes your enemies."

"What about you? Did you name yourself?"

"No," I say. "Never. The world named me. War named me."

"Did Kat give you a name?"

That stops me.

Her voice. Clear. Like she's right beside me.

"Captain America," I say. Quiet. "She called me that."

The boy tilts his head.

"So that’s your name? Captain America."

"No," I say. "It’s not a real name."

"It sounds real."

"It’s a joke. A nickname. Something she said when I was being stupid, or trying too hard to be noble."

"So not your name."

"No."

"Then what is?"

"I don’t know. Something lost. Something I don’t need anymore."

He nods, quiet.

"How else do names happen?"

"Events. Titles. Soldiers give each other names. Nicknames. Dumb ones. They name their squads. Their rifles. Their dicks."

He snorts. "Yours had a name?"

I laugh. "Maybe. If it did, I don’t remember it."

He grins, wide. The first time I've seen that kind of light in his face.

"And the sword?"

That sobers me.

"Yeah," I say. "That one had a name. I just can’t touch it now. It’s in there, swirling. But it’s gone. Doesn’t matter."

"So what name mattered most?"

I don't even hesitate.

"Kat."

He studies me.

"You loved her."

"Yeah. I still do."

He reaches out. Gently. Touches his hand to my temple.

And then I see it all. Every moment. Every laugh. Every wound. The way she moved. The way she cursed. The way she let me stay broken but never let me lie about it.

When he pulls his hand back, his face is different. Older. Sadder. Wiser.

"That’s it," he says. "That’s what fixed you. Love."

"I hardly call this fixed."

"You're still here. Love did that."

"Love caused this," I said. "Or the opposite. Hate, maybe."

"They are the same thing," the boy says in a moment of clarity that shakes me. "We just have to point in the right direction?"

"I don't understand."

"Maybe love saves us all."

"Us all?" I say. I look around the void. "It’s just me and you, bud. I have a name I can’t remember. You don’t have one at all."

"You said it didn’t matter if you remembered your name."

"I did."

"It matters that I know mine."

"Does it? In the next few minutes?"

"I think so. But you won't give me a name?"

"No. I don't even know mine."

"Then I guess I'll have to do it myself."

He doesn’t ask permission.

He just closes his eyes.

Takes a breath that doesn’t belong to lungs.

And speaks.

We don’t hear it.

The name isn’t for me. It isn’t for anyone. It’s for the world.

And the world listens.

There’s a moment—half a heartbeat—then a sound I’ve never heard before and will never hear again. Not quite thunder. Not quite breath. More like the idea of something being born.

The rock beneath us pulses. A ring of gold light explodes outward, silent and vast, rippling through the black like it found something to push against. The void flinches.

Then it starts.

The stone stretches. Not cracks—stretches, like skin remembering how to grow. Green veins trace through the dust. A spiral, bright and clean and endless, rises up from where we sit, twisting into the dark. It’s not fire. It’s not code.

It’s form.

He opens his eyes. Doesn’t smile. Doesn’t need to.

I feel it in my chest. Something pulling—not backward, not up. Just… out.

The world isn’t returning.

It’s beginning.

Light doesn’t return. Not at first.

First comes shape. Then distance. Then breath.

The spiral widens. It draws something behind it—form, mass, maybe even time.

The void isn’t replaced. It’s answered.

Mist condenses in the air that wasn’t air before. Pressure builds. The silence doesn’t break—but it shifts. Like it’s listening.

The boy’s name lingers in the bones of the stone beneath us. Not echoing—engraved.

I don’t know what’s being built. But I know it won’t be what came before.

This isn’t resurrection.

It’s invention.

And I know this world won’t look like the last one.

Not because it’s better. Not because it’s worse.

Just because it’s new.

I look down at my palm. Open. Empty.

And for the first time in all my lives, it doesn’t ache for the steel.

It doesn’t ache at all.



####

He’s older now. Not the boy. A man. The same, but more. Beard coming in. Shoulders broader. Still barefoot.

He leads me through the rising green, through the swirl of mist and geometry and something that smells like wild mint. I don’t know how long we walk. Time hasn’t finished rebuilding itself yet.

At the center of it all, we find the tree.

It’s not tall. Not divine. Just a tree. Bark warm and red. Leaves humming like they remember sunlight. And at its center, low enough for anyone to reach—apples.

He picks one. Turns to me.

“Didn’t end well last time someone ate one of these,” I say.

He shrugs.

“That’s what they want you to believe. They were always wrong."

I smirk, despite myself.

He offers it. I take it. The skin is cool and real in my hand. I don’t bite it right away.

Then I do.

The flesh is sweet. Cold. Alive.

When I look up—

She’s standing there.

Kat.

Barefoot in the grass, shirt loose over one shoulder, an apple already in her hand. She’s mid-chew, like she’s been here the whole time and I’m the one who arrived late.

“Took you long enough,” she says.

I step toward her. She doesn’t run. Doesn’t cry.

We don’t say anything for a while. Just stand there, breathing the same air again. The only kind that’s ever mattered.

Then she touches my face. Thumb across my cheek. Not a wipe. Just a mark. Like she’s checking I’m still real.

“I love you,” she says.

“I know. I've always known."

"You're a big dumb lug," she says, kissing me again. "But I can’t be your Eve.”

“What?”

She grins. That grin.

“We got the same plumbing, baby. Tab A into Tab A doesn’t make babies. You want a new world, we’re gonna need a Slot B somewhere. It's just the way it works."

I open my mouth. Close it. Laugh.

The man laughs too, arms folded across his chest.

"That's the way it worked. Yeah. But not this time. This time, let's try something different. I think the women earned a break this time around.”

“Yeah?” Kat says. She leans in, bumping her shoulder into mine.

"Yeah," the man says with a smile.

She steps back, eyes wide with sudden, absurd joy.

“I’m gonna have lots and lots of babies,” she says.

I blink.

“What?”

She places my hand over her belly.

“You heard me, Patrick Riley Blake. We are going to have lots and lots of babies."

And I remember.

Not as a flash. Not as pain.

Just a name.

Mine.
